text: Assistant: Both repos are clear. Trellis is a **deterministic reconciliation engine** — state in, effect plans + receipts out, with an explicit design goal of being "legible to tooling and AI agents that need to reason about why a system did what it did, without parsing logs." tenex-edge is a Rust/Nostr daemon that **derives live resources** (relay subscriptions, 30315 status events, presence, hook context) from changing state. That's a promising surface. Let me dig into the real tenex-edge code with parallel Sonnet agents. [...] The feedback is a genuine sharpening, not a competing view. The epic already said "the host owns all I/O" but never named the *boundary principle* that makes that non-arbitrary — **Trellis owns decisions; the host owns observations and effects.** That framing does real work: it gives an unambiguous test for what's a canonical input (a fact the world hands you: process died, LLM returned text, relay accepted the event, clock ticked) versus what Trellis derives (session is `working`, this exact 30315 should publish, this subscription should close). It's also fully consistent with the existing cost-model caveat about keeping raw transcripts/event bodies out of the graph. [...] - **Architectural boundary & end-state** — *Trellis owns decisions; the host owns observations and effects.* The world's facts (`ProcessExited`, `DistillCompleted`, `RelayPublishAccepted`, `ClockTick`) enter as a single input journal; Trellis derives sessions/status/who/context/outbox/subscriptions; SQLite becomes a durable log + projection store. Raw transcripts/event bodies stay out — pointers and hashes only. [...] The split-brain point is the most valuable part and was genuinely missing: partial adoption is strictly *worse* than the status quo, because two systems then disagree about the same rows and each believes it's authoritative. That's not a nuance — it's a hard constraint on how slices promote. [...] - **Split-brain risk** — partial adoption is *worse* than today, so each slice must convert **every** writer of a surface in the same change. Shadow mode has no split-brain (nothing is applied); the hazard lives only at promotion — which is exactly why #205 deletes the bespoke path when the close plan turns on, and #206 collapses all triggers at once rather than incrementally. [...] 1. **"Architectural boundary & end-state"** (new, after *Direction*): states the decision/observation/effect boundary and the one-directional loop; lists the facts Trellis must NOT invent (mapped to the proposed input-journal event names: `ProcessExited`, `DistillCompleted`, `RelayPublishAccepted`, `ClockTick`, etc.) vs. the facts Trellis should own; describes the end-state where SQLite becomes a durable log + projection store fed by one input journal; and reuses the epic's existing "pointers/hashes/summaries, not a blob store" rule so it doesn't contradict the cost model. Closes by framing full authoritative state as an **adoption/risk** question, which hands off cleanly to the shadow-mode section.
